Sometimes the first verb does not give information about the subject - it says youto see-a-soUdtor-.) or                                                                  not I suggest that you see a solicitor,  I suggest seeing a solicitor, (     J suggest not We're thinking of moving, (     We're thinking to move.) not It's not very easy to stop smoking, (     ...■  to-stop- to-smoke:) Can I help wash the dishes?

with a difference of meaning or use. For each verb, it is necessary to know which clauses. Many verbs can be followed by more than one of these structures, often followed by -ing forms with or without a preposition (►   100), and some by can be followed by infinitives with or without to (►   97-98), some verbs can be Different structures are possible, depending on the particular verb. Some verbs I hope to see you soon. I enjoy playing cards. I saw that she was crying.

refers to the action. The  second verb structure is often rather like the direct our attitude to an action: the first verb describes the attitude and the second structures including other verbs). He is not swimming      He doesn't like swimming.

He is swimming      - He likes swimming.

Should she understand?  Does she seem to understand?

- She should understand. - She seems to understand.

verb structures the auxiliary do has to be added to the first verb. Compare: verb structures, questions and negatives are made without do; in other verb + called 'auxiliary verbs'. One important difference is grammatical. In auxiliary Other verbs (e.g. seem) which  are used in verb + verb structures are not usually 4 other     verb    + verb    structures For details, ►    Section 7.

She may be in Mexico. You must  call Uncle Arthur.

various meanings, mostly to do with certainty or obligation.

usually called 'modal auxiliary verbs'. They  are used with other verbs to add The  verbs will, shall, would, should, can, could, may, might, must and ought are 3 modal     auxiliary       verbs Section1 Verbs grammar •  16   verb + verb: other structures For tha t-clauses after verbs, ►    258-259, 264.

Nobody told me that you were here.

When are you going to get the clock repaired?

We've got to stop him  making a fool of himself.

We all want you to be happy. (     We   all want that you are happy.) I'd like you to meet Sophie.

Many verbs can be followed by an object as well as a verb structure.
